---
title: "Organizing and Documenting Data Availability"
subtitle: "Using R to Audit Your Data"
author: "Pragati Dahal"
institute: "Virginia Tech · Data Science for the Public Good"
date: "June 5, 2026"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: simple
    logo: images/kohlcentre_logo.png
    footer: "DSPG Summer 2026 · Virginia Tech"
    slide-number: true
    transition: fade
    background-transition: fade
    highlight-style: github
    code-line-numbers: false
    fontsize: 1.1em
    execute:
      eval: false
      echo: true
    css: workshop_styles.css
---

## What Are We Building Today? {background-color="#861F41"}

::: {style="color: white; font-size: 0.95em;"}
By the end of this workshop, you will be able to:

<br>

**Identify** which variables exist in your dataset across years

**Audit** data availability programmatically in R

**Document** your findings in a structured format

**Visualize** the results as an interactive dashboard
:::

::: {.notes}
Open the finished HTML dashboard in browser before starting. Show it here.
:::

---

## Where This Fits {background-color="#ffffff"}

::: {style="font-size: 0.9em;"}
**Before you clean your data, you need to know what you actually have.**

If a variable only exists for 3 out of 14 years — you need to know that *before* you spend hours cleaning it.
:::

<br>

::: {style="font-size: 1em; text-align: center;"}
Raw Data → **[YOU ARE HERE]** → Cleaning → Analysis → Report
:::

<br>

::: {style="font-size: 0.85em; color: #861F41;"}
Today's focus: the step between downloading data and cleaning it.
:::

---

## tidycensus: Your Data Access Tool {background-color="#ffffff"}

::: {style="font-size: 0.85em;"}
You set up your Census API key in the previous workshop. Now we use it.

`tidycensus` gives you ACS data directly in R — no manual downloading.
:::

```r
library(tidycensus)
library(tidyverse)

get_acs(
  geography = "county",      # unit of analysis
  variables = "B19013_001",  # which variable
  state     = "VA",          # which state
  year      = 2022,          # which year
  survey    = "acs5"         # 5-year estimates
)
```

---

## Finding Variable Codes {background-color="#ffffff"}

::: {style="font-size: 0.85em;"}
ACS variables follow a pattern: **Table + Cell number**

`B19013_001` = Table B19013 (Median Household Income), Cell 001
:::

<br>

```r
# Search the full variable list
vars <- load_variables(2022, "acs5", cache = TRUE)
View(vars)

# Search by keyword
vars |>
  filter(str_detect(label, "income|Income")) |>
  select(name, label, concept)
```

::: {style="font-size: 0.8em; color: #E5751F;"}
💡 Always check the `concept` column — it tells you which table the variable belongs to.
:::

---

## Your First Data Pull {background-color="#ffffff"}

```r
va_income <- get_acs(
  geography = "county",
  variables = "B19013_001",
  state     = "VA",
  year      = 2022,
  survey    = "acs5"
)

head(va_income)
```

::: {style="font-size: 0.78em; background: #f3eeea; padding: 12px; border-radius: 6px; font-family: monospace;"}
\# A tibble: 6 × 5<br>
&nbsp;&nbsp;GEOID &nbsp;NAME &nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp; variable &nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;estimate &nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;moe<br>
1 51001 &nbsp;Accomack County, Virginia &nbsp;&nbsp; B19013_001 &nbsp;&nbsp;52694 &nbsp;&nbsp;5883<br>
2 51003 &nbsp;Albemarle County, Virginia &nbsp; B19013_001 &nbsp;&nbsp;97708 &nbsp;&nbsp;3686
:::

---

## Pulling Multiple Years {background-color="#ffffff"}

::: {style="font-size: 0.85em;"}
Use `map_dfr()` to loop over years and stack the results into one dataframe:
:::

```r
years <- 2015:2022

multi_year <- map_dfr(years, function(yr) {
  get_acs(
    geography = "county",
    variables = c(
      median_income = "B19013_001",
      poverty       = "B17001_002"
    ),
    state  = "VA",
    year   = yr,
    survey = "acs5"
  ) |>
    mutate(year = yr)
})
```

::: {style="font-size: 0.8em; color: #861F41;"}
`map_dfr()` = run this for each year, then stack all results into one dataframe.
:::

---

## The Problem: Not All Variables Exist {background-color="#ffffff"}

::: {style="font-size: 0.85em;"}
Try pulling broadband access for 2012:
:::

```r
get_acs(
  geography = "county",
  variables = "B28002_004",  # broadband access
  state     = "VA",
  year      = 2012,
  survey    = "acs5"
)
```

::: {style="font-size: 0.85em; margin-top: 20px;"}
**You get an error.** ACS didn't track broadband at the county level before 2017.

This is not a data error — it's measurement history. And you need to know this *before* you build your analysis.
:::

---

## The Audit Function {background-color="#ffffff"}

::: {style="font-size: 0.85em;"}
Use `tryCatch()` to handle errors gracefully — returns `TRUE` if data exists, `FALSE` if not:
:::

```r
check_variable <- function(var, year) {
  tryCatch({
    result <- get_acs(
      geography = "county",
      variables = var,
      state     = "VA",
      year      = year,
      survey    = "acs5"
    )
    nrow(result) > 0
  }, error = function(e) FALSE)
}

check_variable("B28002_004", 2012)  # FALSE
check_variable("B28002_004", 2020)  # TRUE
```

---

## Running the Full Audit {background-color="#ffffff"}

```r
vars <- c(
  "B19013_001",  "B17001_002",  "B15003_022",
  "B27001_001",  "B01003_001",  "B28002_004"
)

years <- 2010:2023

audit_results <- expand_grid(variable = vars, year = years) |>
  mutate(available = map2_lgl(variable, year, check_variable))
```

::: {style="font-size: 0.8em; color: #E5751F; margin-top: 16px;"}
⚠️ This makes ~84 API calls — takes a few minutes. Save immediately:

`write_csv(audit_results, "audit_results.csv")`
:::

---

## What the Audit Reveals {background-color="#ffffff"}

| Variable | Coverage | Missing |
|----------|----------|---------|
| Total Population | ✅ 14/14 | — |
| Median Household Income | ✅ 14/14 | — |
| Population Below Poverty | ✅ 14/14 | — |
| Bachelor's Degree | ⚠️ 12/14 | 2010–2011 |
| Health Insurance Universe | ⚠️ 12/14 | 2010–2011 |
| **Broadband Access** | **⚠️ 7/14** | **2010–2016** |

::: {style="font-size: 0.8em; color: #861F41; margin-top: 12px;"}
If your project needs broadband data, your analysis window starts in 2017 — not 2010.
:::

---

## Adding Labels and Categories {background-color="#ffffff"}

::: {style="font-size: 0.82em;"}
Variable codes aren't human-readable. Add your own labels and categories:
:::

```r
var_metadata <- tibble(
  variable = c("B19013_001","B17001_002","B15003_022",
               "B27001_001","B01003_001","B28002_004"),
  label    = c("Median Household Income","Population Below Poverty",
               "Bachelor's Degree","Health Insurance Universe",
               "Total Population","Broadband Access"),
  category = c("Socioeconomic","Socioeconomic","Socioeconomic",
               "Healthcare Access","Demographics","Infrastructure")
)

audit_final <- audit_results |>
  left_join(var_metadata, by = "variable") |>
  group_by(variable, label, category) |>
  summarise(
    n_years       = sum(available),
    years_present = list(year[available == TRUE]),
    missing_years = list(year[available == FALSE]),
    .groups = "drop"
  )
```

---

## Building the Dashboard {background-color="#ffffff"}

::: {style="font-size: 0.85em;"}
**Option 1 — Pure R** using `reactable`:
:::

```r
library(reactable)
library(htmlwidgets)

widget <- reactable(audit_final, filterable = TRUE, searchable = TRUE)
saveWidget(widget, "data_availability.html")
```

::: {style="font-size: 0.85em; margin-top: 16px;"}
**Option 2 — R + Claude** for a more polished result:
:::

```r
library(jsonlite)
cat(toJSON(audit_final |>
  mutate(years_present = map(years_present, as.integer),
         missing_years = map(missing_years, as.integer)),
  pretty = TRUE))
```

::: {style="font-size: 0.8em; color: #861F41;"}
Copy the JSON → paste into Claude with a prompt → download HTML
:::

---

## Now You Try {background-color="#E5751F"}

::: {style="color: white; font-size: 0.95em;"}
**Your turn — 20 minutes**

<br>

1. Open `workshop_exercise.R` from the shared folder
2. `acs_virginia_raw.csv` is pre-loaded — no API calls needed
3. Fill in the **3 gaps** marked `# YOUR CODE HERE`
4. Run the audit and build your dashboard
5. If you finish early — try Option 2 with Claude

<br>

*Solution is in `workshop_solution.R` if you get stuck.*
:::

---

## Apply This to Your Project {background-color="#ffffff"}

::: {style="font-size: 0.9em;"}
1. **Read your project proposal** — what variables do you need?
2. **Find the ACS codes** using `load_variables()`
3. **Run the audit** — which years are actually available?
4. **Add your own labels and categories**
5. **Generate the dashboard** — share with your team and stakeholders
6. **Save the script** — this is part of your project documentation
:::

::: {style="font-size: 0.8em; color: #E5751F; margin-top: 16px;"}
Rural Health & AVI teams: your broadband analysis window starts in 2017, not 2010.
:::

---

## Summary {background-color="#861F41"}

::: {style="color: white; font-size: 0.9em;"}
**What you learned today:**

- How to pull ACS data by variable code using `tidycensus`
- How to audit variable availability across years with `tryCatch()`
- Why some variables have gaps — and why that matters
- How to document your data with labels and categories
- How to turn your audit into a shareable HTML dashboard

<br>

**The pipeline:**

`get_acs()` → `check_variable()` → `expand_grid()` → `reactable` / Claude → HTML
:::

---

## Questions? {background-color="#ffffff"}

::: {style="text-align: center; margin-top: 40px;"}
**Pragati Dahal**

Graduate Research Assistant · Agricultural & Applied Economics

Virginia Tech

[pdahal23@vt.edu](mailto:pdahal23@vt.edu)

<br>

*Workshop materials available in the shared DSPG folder*
:::
